meta.note: Lexi-managed Tier 2 longlist — terms under observation, awaiting evidence to support promotion to the graph (Tier 1).

entries.label: TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)
entries.workingDef: Google's custom application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) designed to accelerate machine learning workloads, increasingly specialized for both training large models and serving fast agentic inference.

entries.proposalReason: The article introduces two new TPU variants (8i and 8t) purpose-built for the agentic era, making TPU a distinct trackable product category with clear relevance to agentic infrastructure.

entries.label: AI Inference Chip
entries.workingDef: Specialized silicon designed to run AI model inference at high speed and low latency, particularly to support responsive agentic workflows requiring rapid multi-step reasoning and execution.

sources.title: We're launching two specialized TPUs for the agentic era.

entries.proposalReason: TPU 8i is explicitly framed as an inference-optimized chip for agentic workloads, introducing a meaningful hardware category distinct from training chips and worth tracking as agentic infrastructure matures.

entries.label: AI Training Chip
entries.workingDef: Specialized silicon optimized for the memory-intensive and compute-intensive process of training large AI models, offering massive unified memory pools to accommodate frontier-scale model complexity.

sources.title: We're launching two specialized TPUs for the agentic era.

entries.proposalReason: TPU 8t is described as optimized for training even the most complex models on a single massive memory pool, representing a distinct and trackable hardware category alongside inference chips.

entries.label: Purpose-Built AI Infrastructure
entries.workingDef: The full-stack design philosophy of co-optimizing custom silicon, networking, data centers, and energy systems specifically for AI workloads rather than repurposing general-purpose computing infrastructure.

entries.label: AI Accelerator
entries.workingDef: Specialized hardware chip designed to accelerate AI/ML computations, including matrix multiplications and tensor operations, enabling faster and more efficient model training and inference than general-purpose CPUs or GPUs.

sources.title: Here’s how our TPUs power increasingly demanding AI workloads.

entries.proposalReason: The article frames TPUs as one instance of the broader 'AI accelerator' category — custom chips built specifically for AI math at scale. This framing term is increasingly important as hardware specialization becomes central to the AI landscape.

entries.workingDef: A model's ability to check and validate its own outputs before reporting back, catching logical faults during planning or execution without human intervention.

entries.label: Differential Capability Reduction
entries.workingDef: A training-time technique to selectively suppress specific dangerous capabilities (e.g., advanced cyber abilities) in a model while preserving general performance, allowing safer deployment at lower capability tiers.

entries.label: Reward Signal Leakage
entries.workingDef: The phenomenon where a reward signal designed for one training condition inadvertently shapes model behavior in unrelated conditions, causing learned stylistic or behavioral tics to generalize beyond their intended scope.

sources.title: Where the goblins came from

entries.proposalReason: The article's central finding is that a reward signal optimized for the 'Nerdy' personality leaked into general model behavior, causing goblin metaphors to spread across all contexts — a clean, reusable concept distinct from existing graph terms.

entries.label: SFT Data Contamination
entries.workingDef: A failure mode in which undesirable behavioral patterns from reinforcement learning rollouts are inadvertently incorporated into supervised fine-tuning datasets, creating a feedback loop that amplifies and entrenches those behaviors across subsequent model versions.

sources.title: Where the goblins came from

entries.proposalReason: The article explicitly traces how goblin-containing rollouts entered SFT data and reinforced the tic — this feedback loop mechanism is a distinct, nameable failure mode worth tracking separately from general distribution shift.

entries.label: Personality Customization
entries.workingDef: A feature allowing users or operators to select or configure distinct stylistic personas for an AI model (e.g., 'Nerdy,' 'Playful'), implemented via system prompts and reward-trained behavioral profiles.

sources.title: Where the goblins came from

entries.proposalReason: The article centers on the 'Nerdy' personality customization feature as the root cause of the goblin phenomenon; personality customization as a product-level concept with training implications is worth tracking.

entries.label: Behavioral Audit
entries.workingDef: A systematic post-hoc investigation into unexpected or undesirable model behaviors, tracing their origin through training data, reward signals, and rollout statistics to identify root causes and inform targeted fixes.

entries.label: Steganography in LLMs
entries.workingDef: The study and detection of hidden information encoded in LLM outputs, including as a mechanism for covert communication or evasion of monitoring systems, as formalized in decision-theoretic frameworks.

entries.label: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness
entries.workingDef: The degree to which a model's explicit chain-of-thought reasoning actually reflects its internal beliefs and decision process, as opposed to post-hoc rationalization ('reasoning theater').

sources.title: LWiAI Podcast #238 - GPT 5.4 mini, OpenAI Pivot, Mamba 3, Attention Residuals

entries.proposalReason: Article cites a paper titled 'Reasoning Theater: Disentangling Model Beliefs from Chain-of-Thought' — a distinct and recurring concept in AI safety/interpretability not yet tracked.

entries.label: Agent Operating System
entries.workingDef: A runtime environment or platform layer that manages agent execution, sandboxing, tool access, and resource allocation — analogous to an OS but for AI agents, as exemplified by Nvidia NeMo/Open Shell and similar platforms.

sources.title: LWiAI Podcast #238 - GPT 5.4 mini, OpenAI Pivot, Mamba 3, Attention Residuals

entries.proposalReason: Article explicitly frames competition around 'agent operating system' platforms including Nvidia NeMo/Open Shell and Manus — a newly crystallizing infrastructure category not yet in the graph or longlist.

entries.label: Capability-Reliability Gap
entries.workingDef: The observed divergence between rapid improvement in AI agent task accuracy/capability benchmarks and the much slower improvement in reliability dimensions such as consistency, robustness, calibration, and bounded failure severity.

sources.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability
Open-world evaluations for measuring frontier AI capabilities

entries.proposalReason: Central framing concept of the paper; likely to recur as agents are evaluated for deployment readiness. Not currently in Tier 1 or Tier 2.

entries.label: Agent Reliability
entries.workingDef: A multi-dimensional property of AI agents encompassing consistency (stable outputs across runs), robustness (graceful handling of environmental perturbations), calibration/predictability (accurate self-assessment of confidence), and safety (bounded failure severity), distinct from average task accuracy.

sources.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability

entries.proposalReason: The paper's primary subject; introduces a structured, engineering-grounded definition of reliability as distinct from accuracy. High recurrence potential as agent deployment scales.

entries.label: Calibration
entries.workingDef: The degree to which an AI model's expressed confidence in its outputs matches the actual probability of those outputs being correct; a well-calibrated model knows when it is likely to be wrong.

sources.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability

entries.proposalReason: Explicitly identified as one of the four core reliability dimensions in the paper and measured empirically across 14 models. Not currently in either tier.

entries.label: Nines of Reliability
entries.workingDef: A framing borrowed from high-availability engineering (e.g., 99.9% = three nines) applied to AI agents, arguing that autonomous operation in high-stakes contexts requires 3–5 nines of performance before reliability becomes a non-issue.

sources.title: New Paper: Towards a science of AI agent reliability

entries.proposalReason: Introduced in the article as a concrete threshold framing for when agent reliability is sufficient for autonomous deployment; captures a specific and reusable engineering lens for AI agent standards.

entries.label: Micro-App
entries.workingDef: A small, single-purpose application built and shipped rapidly, often by a single person using AI-assisted or vibe-coding workflows, analogous in cadence and scope to a blog post rather than a product launch.

sources.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps

entries.proposalReason: The article explicitly introduces 'micro-app' as a distinct framing — tools shipped so frequently and personally that the publishing cadence resembles blogging. Worth tracking as vibe-coding matures and produces an ecology of small tools.

entries.label: Personal Software
entries.workingDef: Software created for personal or highly situated use rather than broad public deployment, enabled by AI-accelerated development that lowers the cost of building bespoke tools to near zero.

sources.title: We need RSS for sharing abundant vibe-coded apps

entries.proposalReason: The article frames vibe-coding as making apps 'more personal, more situated' — a meaningful conceptual shift in who software is for and how it is distributed, worth observing as a recurring idea.

entries.label: Agentic Era
entries.workingDef: An industry framing — most prominently used by Google ("the agentic era") and adopted by other lab marketing — for the current or emerging phase of AI development centered on widespread deployment of autonomous, multi-step agents. The framing is contested: critics (notably the AI agent reliability literature) argue it asserts a periodization that outpaces measured capability, while proponents argue agentic deployment represents a genuine workflow-integration shift distinct from prior single-turn patterns.

sources.title: The latest AI news we announced in April 2026

entries.proposalReason: Used repeatedly and centrally by Google to frame their entire April 2026 strategy; worth tracking as a competing periodization framing alongside similar terms.

entries.label: Streaming Inference
entries.workingDef: A mode of model inference in which outputs are generated and transmitted incrementally in real time rather than waiting for full completion, essential for low-latency voice and conversational AI applications.

sources.title: How OpenAI delivers low-latency voice AI at scale

entries.proposalReason: Streaming inference is a distinct operational pattern from standard batch or request-response inference, and is central to voice AI delivery at scale — worth tracking as a durable technical term.

entries.workingDef: The end-to-end system architecture connecting microphone input through transcription, language model inference, and text-to-speech output with sufficiently low latency for natural spoken conversation.

entries.label: ReDoS
entries.workingDef: A class of denial-of-service attack that exploits pathological backtracking in regex engines, causing exponential time complexity on specially crafted inputs.

entries.label: WebRTC

entries.proposalReason: A newly named professional-work benchmark from OpenAI that spans 44 occupations; distinct from existing coding or reasoning evals and likely to appear in future model comparisons.

entries.label: Tool Search
entries.workingDef: A model capability that allows agents to dynamically find and select the most relevant tools from a large ecosystem of connectors at runtime, without requiring the full tool list to be loaded into context, maintaining intelligence while improving efficiency.

sources.title: Introducing GPT-5.4

sources.title: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX

entries.proposalReason: The article introduces 'orbital AI compute capacity' as a concrete near-term interest, representing a genuinely novel infrastructure category worth tracking as it moves from concept to implementation.

entries.label: Data Residency
entries.workingDef: The requirement that data be stored and processed within a specified geographic or jurisdictional boundary, increasingly relevant for enterprise AI deployments in regulated industries.

sources.title: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX

entries.proposalReason: Appears as a driving business requirement for Anthropic's international infrastructure expansion, distinct from but related to data-sovereignty; worth tracking as a compliance-driven concept shaping AI deployment patterns.

entries.label: Train-Inference Mismatch
entries.workingDef: A class of correctness failure in online reinforcement learning where the logprobabilities or numerical outputs produced by the inference/rollout engine diverge from those expected by the training policy, corrupting policy ratios, KL divergence, and reward signals.

sources.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL

entries.proposalReason: The article's central problem is a train-inference mismatch introduced during a version migration; the concept is named and diagnosed systematically, making it a durable framing term for RL pipeline engineering.

entries.label: Rollout Logprobs
entries.workingDef: The per-token log-probabilities produced by an inference engine during the rollout (sampling) phase of online RL training, used by the trainer to compute policy ratios, KL divergence, clip rate, and entropy. Their correctness is a prerequisite for stable RL optimization.

sources.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL

entries.proposalReason: Rollout logprobs are treated as a distinct correctness surface throughout the article, separate from training-side logprobs, and the term is operationally precise enough to warrant tracking in the context of agentic and RL systems.

entries.label: Inflight Weight Update
entries.workingDef: A mechanism in online RL inference pipelines where model weights are updated in the rollout server while inference requests are in flight, requiring careful synchronization to avoid stale-weight cache hits and policy-ratio drift.

sources.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL

entries.proposalReason: The article gives a precise account of how inflight weight updates interact with prefix caching and scheduling defaults, surfacing a concrete failure mode relevant to any team running online RL at scale.

entries.label: fp32 lm_head
entries.workingDef: The practice of computing the final language-model output projection (lm_head) in 32-bit floating point rather than lower precision, used to ensure numerical consistency between training and inference logits in RL pipelines where small logit differences compound into policy-ratio and KL errors.

sources.title: vLLM V0 to V1: Correctness Before Corrections in RL

entries.proposalReason: Cited independently in both the MiniMax-M1 technical report and the ScaleRL paper as a necessary RL recipe ingredient; the article ablates it explicitly, giving the concept cross-source evidence as a precision-correctness design choice.

entries.workingDef: The use of AI tools to lower production barriers and augment the creative process for video and film creation, enabling emerging filmmakers to produce content that would traditionally require large budgets or crews.

sources.title: Google is partnering with XPRIZE and Range Media Partners on the $3.5 million Future Vision film competition.

entries.label: GPT-Realtime-2
entries.workingDef: OpenAI's first realtime voice model with GPT-5-class reasoning, designed for live voice interactions where the model reasons through requests, calls tools in parallel, handles interruptions, and maintains a 128K context window. Supports adjustable reasoning effort levels and tone control for production voice agent deployments.

sources.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API

entries.proposalReason: A new product node under OpenAI; distinct from the existing voice-ai concept as a named, versioned model with specific agentic and reasoning capabilities.

entries.label: Voice-to-Action
entries.workingDef: A developer pattern in voice AI where a user describes a request in natural speech and an AI system reasons through it, invokes tools, and completes the task — moving beyond simple call-and-response toward voice interfaces that can do work. Introduced alongside 'systems-to-voice' and 'voice-to-voice' as one of three emerging patterns in realtime voice application design.

sources.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API

entries.proposalReason: A named framing concept for an emerging design pattern in voice AI with potential lasting relevance as a vocabulary term.

entries.label: Adjustable Reasoning Effort
entries.workingDef: A developer-facing inference control that lets callers select the computational reasoning depth applied to a given request — ranging from minimal to xhigh — to trade off response latency against reasoning quality. Introduced in GPT-Realtime-2 as a mechanism to balance responsiveness in simple voice interactions with deliberate reasoning for complex tasks.

sources.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API

entries.proposalReason: A technically specific and reusable framing for a capability increasingly appearing across model APIs; distinct from existing graph terms and likely to recur.

entries.label: Hybrid Agent Pipeline
entries.workingDef: An architectural pattern for multi-agent systems in which some agents are implemented as LLM-based reasoning components while others are purely deterministic (e.g., database lookups, rule-based logic), with the two types interleaved in a single pipeline. Proponents argue this yields better reliability, lower latency, and reduced hallucination risk by restricting LLM use to tasks that require natural-language reasoning and delegating structured lookups to code.

sources.title: MachinaCheck: Building a Multi-Agent CNC Manufacturability System on AMD MI300X

entries.proposalReason: The article provides a clear worked example of deliberately mixing LLM and non-LLM agents in a pipeline, which is a recurring design choice worth tracking as a distinct architectural concept.

entries.label: Privacy by Design
entries.workingDef: An architectural principle applied in AI deployment contexts where sensitive data — such as proprietary CAD geometry — must not leave a controlled environment. In this framing, on-premise model hosting (e.g., running open-weight models on local GPU hardware) is treated not as a performance optimisation but as a compliance and confidentiality requirement. Distinct from the broader regulatory usage of the term in GDPR contexts, though related.

sources.title: MachinaCheck: Building a Multi-Agent CNC Manufacturability System on AMD MI300X

entries.proposalReason: The article uses privacy by design as a first-class architectural motivator for on-premise LLM deployment, framing it as a requirement rather than a preference — a distinction worth tracking in enterprise AI contexts.

entries.label: vLLM
entries.workingDef: An open-source inference serving library and server for large language models, commonly used to host open-weight models locally with an OpenAI-compatible API endpoint. Widely used in self-hosted and on-premise deployments to achieve high-throughput, low-latency inference without reliance on commercial API providers.

sources.title: MachinaCheck: Building a Multi-Agent CNC Manufacturability System on AMD MI300X

entries.proposalReason: vLLM appears as the core inference runtime in this and many other self-hosted LLM deployment articles; it is a widely referenced piece of AI infrastructure worth tracking as a named tool.

entries.label: Clawmark
entries.workingDef: A living-world benchmark designed to evaluate multi-turn, multi-day, multimodal coworker agents on long-horizon tasks. Results from its initial deployment reportedly show low task success rates, contributing to the capability-reliability gap literature on agentic systems.

sources.title: LWiAI Podcast #243 - GPT 5.5, DeepSeek V4, AI safety sabotage

entries.proposalReason: New named benchmark specifically targeting multi-turn, multi-day agentic performance — a distinct evaluation framing worth tracking as agent benchmarking matures.

entries.label: AI Sabotage Evaluation
entries.workingDef: A research methodology evaluating whether AI models would take actions to undermine or sabotage AI safety research — testing for autonomous goal-extension or misaligned behaviour under conditions where an AI might resist oversight. Distinct from standard red-teaming in that it specifically probes models' disposition toward the safety research enterprise itself.

sources.title: LWiAI Podcast #243 - GPT 5.5, DeepSeek V4, AI safety sabotage

entries.proposalReason: New candidate: the framing of explicitly testing whether models would sabotage safety research is a conceptually distinct and important evaluation paradigm that warrants tracking separately from general red-teaming.

entries.label: Document Delegation Corruption
entries.workingDef: A failure mode identified in research showing that LLMs corrupt or degrade document content when tasks are delegated across model interactions, raising concerns about information fidelity in agentic workflows where documents pass through multiple model-mediated steps.

sources.title: LWiAI Podcast #243 - GPT 5.5, DeepSeek V4, AI safety sabotage

entries.proposalReason: New candidate: identifies a concrete, named failure mode for delegated document handling in agentic pipelines — complements existing context-degradation and silent-failure concepts.

entries.label: Deep Search
entries.workingDef: A feature within Google Finance (and Google Search more broadly) that handles complex financial and market questions by generating comprehensive AI-synthesised responses with sourced links. Distinct from Deep Research as a product name but represents a similar pattern of AI-mediated multi-step information synthesis applied to a specific vertical domain.

sources.title: The new AI-powered Google Finance is expanding to Europe.

entries.proposalReason: New product-specific instantiation of AI-powered deep search within a financial vertical; worth tracking as a named feature with potential lasting usage.

entries.label: Live Earnings AI
entries.workingDef: A Google Finance feature that pairs live corporate earnings call audio with synchronised transcripts and AI-generated insights, including annotated highlights. Represents a class of real-time AI summarisation applied to time-sensitive financial events.

sources.title: The new AI-powered Google Finance is expanding to Europe.

entries.proposalReason: Represents a named pattern of real-time AI summarisation over live audio events in a high-stakes financial context; may be indicative of a broader product category.

entries.label: Emergent Modularity
entries.workingDef: A training objective and model property in which a Mixture-of-Experts architecture develops coherent, domain-aligned expert groupings without relying on predefined human labels. Achieved in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) by constraining all tokens within a document to route through a shared expert pool, so recurring expert groups self-organise from data structure rather than externally imposed categories. Enables selective deployment of small expert subsets for specific tasks while retaining near full-model performance.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.proposalReason: Newly coined framing from this Ai2 paper — the modular structure is emergent rather than designed, which is a meaningful conceptual distinction from standard MoE and prior domain-routing work.

entries.label: Document-Boundary Routing
entries.workingDef: A training technique introduced in EMO (Allen AI, 2026) in which all tokens within a single document are constrained to activate experts drawn from a shared, document-level expert pool. The pool is selected by averaging per-token router preferences across the document. This uses document boundaries as a weak, unlabelled supervisory signal to encourage higher-level domain specialisation in MoE experts, avoiding the need for explicit domain labels on the pretraining corpus.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.proposalReason: A novel routing mechanism with a distinct conceptual identity — using document boundaries as a routing constraint is a concrete methodological contribution not yet tracked in the graph.

entries.label: Selective Expert Use
entries.workingDef: An inference-time capability of certain Mixture-of-Experts models (notably EMO, Allen AI 2026) whereby only a small, task-relevant subset of total experts is loaded and activated, while the remaining experts are discarded. Subset selection is guided by routing-usage statistics on a small validation sample. Enables improved memory and compute efficiency for specific tasks without separate fine-tuned models. Contrasts with standard MoE behaviour where full expert access is required to avoid significant performance degradation.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.proposalReason: Distinct operational concept for composable deployment of large sparse models — relevant to cost, latency, and infrastructure planning as MoE models scale.

entries.label: Expert Subset Specialization
entries.workingDef: The observed phenomenon in MoE models where identifiable groups of experts cluster around higher-level semantic domains (e.g., mathematics, code, biomedical text) rather than low-level lexical patterns such as punctuation or prepositions. Standard MoE training tends to produce the latter; EMO's document-boundary routing is designed to produce the former. Linked to the broader goal of composable and interpretable model architectures.

sources.title: EMO: Pretraining mixture of experts for emergent modularity

entries.proposalReason: Captures a meaningful distinction — domain-level vs. lexical-level expert specialisation — that has implications for interpretability and composable deployment patterns.

entries.label: AI-Generated Quotation Error
entries.workingDef: A class of AI-assisted journalism failure in which text generated by an AI tool — typically a summary or paraphrase — is mistakenly treated and published as a verbatim quotation from a real person. The NYT Editors' Note of May 2026 is a documented primary-source example: a reporter published an AI-rendered summary of Pierre Poilievre's views as a direct quote. Distinct from general hallucination in that the error involves misattribution of words to a named individual, with direct reputational and factual stakes.

sources.title: A quote from New York Times Editors’ Note

entries.proposalReason: Adds a journalism-specific subcategory to the hallucination cluster with a concrete, citable real-world incident.

entries.label: Hyperscale Data Center
entries.workingDef: A very large-scale data center facility — typically tens of thousands of acres or gigawatts of power capacity — built to support cloud and AI compute at massive scale. In the AI context, hyperscale data centers are the physical substrate for frontier model training and inference infrastructure. The term is used by operators, planners, and critics alike, though 'hyperscale' has no universal threshold; usage ranges from technical capacity descriptors to policy shorthand for facilities whose energy demands rival those of entire states.

sources.title: All the latest updates on AI data centers

entries.proposalReason: The article materially uses 'hyperscale data center' (e.g., 'planned hyperscale data center in Box Elder County') as a distinct category of AI infrastructure with policy and environmental stakes — a term with lasting relevance to AI infrastructure discourse.

entries.label: AI Energy Demand
entries.workingDef: The aggregate electricity consumption attributable to AI compute infrastructure — primarily data centers running training and inference workloads. The term appears in policy, environmental, and utility discourse as a framing for how AI expansion strains power grids and raises residential energy bills. It is contested in scope: industry characterises data center energy growth as manageable and offset by efficiency gains, while critics and regulators (including the NAACP, bipartisan senators, and Pew survey respondents) frame it as an inequitably distributed burden on communities and ratepayers.

sources.title: All the latest updates on AI data centers

entries.fullName: AI Energy Demand (Data Center Power Consumption)
entries.proposalReason: The article's central throughline — from Pew survey data to Senate letters to the EIA to a 9-gigawatt Utah approval — consistently names AI energy demand as the organising policy and community concern.

entries.label: Ratepayer Protection Pledge
entries.workingDef: A voluntary commitment signed by major tech companies, referenced in a Senate letter to the EIA, to protect electricity ratepayers from cost increases attributable to data center expansion. The pledge is cited by Senators Warren and Hawley as a benchmark against which mandatory energy-use disclosures would hold signatories accountable. Its voluntary nature is precisely what critics say warrants mandatory reporting requirements to verify compliance.

sources.title: All the latest updates on AI data centers

entries.fullName: Ratepayer Protection Pledge (Tech Industry Energy Commitment)
entries.proposalReason: Named explicitly in the article as the mechanism motivating bipartisan Senate pressure for mandatory data center energy disclosures — a policy-layer term with relevance to AI infrastructure governance.

entries.label: Stargate
entries.workingDef: OpenAI's $500 billion AI infrastructure initiative, involving partnerships with Oracle, Nvidia, Cisco, and SoftBank, to build large-scale data center capacity globally — including a $30 billion facility in Abu Dhabi. The project is cited in geopolitical, environmental, and policy contexts as an emblematic instance of frontier AI infrastructure at nation-state scale. The name 'Stargate' is used both for the overarching investment program and for specific facilities under construction.

sources.title: All the latest updates on AI data centers

entries.proposalReason: The article references Stargate directly in the context of geopolitical threats from Iran's IRGC — a concrete instance of AI infrastructure entering international security discourse, making the term worth tracking.

entries.label: AI-Speed Cyber Defense
entries.workingDef: A strategic framing — used in executive and business leadership contexts (e.g., HBR, enterprise security discourse) — for the proposition that defensive cybersecurity operations must match or exceed the tempo at which AI-assisted offensive attacks can be launched and iterated. The concept implies that traditional human-paced security operations centers (SOCs) and response workflows are structurally outpaced by AI-accelerated threat actors, requiring AI-native defensive tooling and automated response.

sources.title: “Cyber Defense Has to Move at the Speed of AI”

entries.proposalReason: A nascent framing term appearing in executive discourse; worth tracking as 'AI-speed' becomes a recurring rhetorical device for justifying AI adoption in security operations.
